item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The number change reminded me that I liked this a lot and forgot to upvote it.

1+

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The number has been changed to allow someone else a series 1 slot, under my own author authority and that which devolved to me when Wilt gave control of his work to Salman and Salman entrusted his articles and all else under his care to me.

item.title: Re: Staff Post - Open
item.content:encoded: I don't mean to be rude but I don't think the picture works for two reasons, one it lacks a plow, and two the things it's carrying don't look like "workable material" but that might be me. Also from what I seen when a image is deleted or changed they say they can't locate the image or it's not cc-compliant, but neither of those are mentioned in this forum, as for all I know you just replace a cc-compliant image that had the plow I previously mentioned and replaced it with your image. If the original picture wasn't cc compliant I think this article would be better with out one.

item.title: Staff Post - Open
item.content:encoded:
The image that was used in this article has been replaced with an image that's cc-compliant with the SCP wiki's license (CC BY-SA 3.0) and is sourced as follows:

Title: Brokengod-fist-new.jpg
Author(s): Joe Ebalo, Elogee FishTruck
Release year: 2020
Note: Created by Elogee FishTruck as a cropped version of the image below

Source: United States Navy
License: Public Domain
Title: 041026-N-1825E-041
Author: Joe Ebalo

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
DUDE!!!!! THAT THING LOOKS SO COOOOL!!!!!!
seriously tho, where did you get this inspiration? it looks like something the Church of the Broken God would make. Upvote+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The first SCP I ever read and it was the perfect one to bring me into this

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Simple and short, but it is so cool… especially the reveal of a really advanced alien civilization.
+1

item.title: Staff Post - Open
item.content:encoded:
Timer Expired. Edits made as discussed below. Author may revert at any time.

The WikiWalk team is proposing edits to this page. All proposed edits will be in a collapsible below this post, and after 7 days if there are no serious objections to the edits, the feedback received will be taken into account as the edits are made. If you have questions not directly related to the article about this project, please pm a member of the WikiWalk Team

+ Show Block
- Hide Block

What a far out skip. In addition to a simple link to the Site-77 Dossier, another crosslink has the potential to exist in this article. Discussions with Roget, has revealed that they personally headcanon that this article is linked to SCP-1683, which is another astronomical skip. As such, I am proposing the following edit:

Changing the following:

The majority of the planetary system models created by SCP-720 do not match other systems currently known to exist. However, at least four systems match up: Sol, Kepler-11, Gliese 876, and Mu Arae. Several of the models seem to attempt to model other known systems, but include disparities such as an incorrect number of planets, the presence of circumstellar disks, or asteroid belts. These models are to be photographed and kept on record, in the event that further information discovered on known systems reveal them to be similar.

To this:

The majority of the planetary system models created by SCP-720 do not match other systems currently known to exist. However, at least four systems match up: Sol, Kepler-11, Gliese 876, and Mu Arae. Several of the models seem to attempt to model other known systems, but include disparities such as an incorrect number of planets, the presence of circumstellar disks, or asteroid belts. These models are to be photographed and kept on record, in the event that further information discovered on known systems reveal them to be similar.

Such a link is non-invasive and opens the door to a lot of potential narrative threads. Perhaps the creator of 1683 is beaming back info to 720, and is the source of the unfamiliar systems, perhaps something a little more weird is going on.

If anyone has other/better examples that could be used here please leave a comment saying so!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like it. It's a neat little thing, and interesting for someone who's into astronomy, like I am.

item.title: Re: 'Sol'
item.content:encoded:
It's useful to use those alternate naming things if you're dealing with multiple stars or star systems- the Sun would denote the primary star of any star system you're in, the Moon would denote the primary moon of the planet you're on, etc. "Sol" and "Luna" would therefore specify Earth's sun and moon.

Not much use here, since such terminology would be only be really useful for any extrasolar Sites the Foundation would have. Which I can't see any indications of in this article.

item.title: 'Sol'
item.content:encoded: I know it's a nitpick but the scientific name for The Sun is 'The Sun'. Just as the name for The Moon is 'The Moon' and Earth is 'Earth'. I don't really know why, but I always get mildly irritated when I see Sol, Luna or Terra.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Thought this one was actually pretty cool.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
5 (five) liter tanks of red, blue, green, and yellow paint

(A minor issue: The 3 primary colors are red, blue, and yellow. You can remove the green tank since the machine can just use blue and yellow and to make green.)

Also, can you add a few lines about its origin? How did the foundation find it? Have they tried to hack into its system to control it? I'm curious.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is a great article, but I'm pretty sure that if you do the number (number) thing, it's supposed to be "seven (7)" and not "7 (seven)". It looks weird this way.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Somewhen, somewhy, a solar system was destroyed. Wiped from existence, burn from all memories. Save one. On a far away planet, for reasons still unknown, a claw-mounted crane was bestowed the memory of the solar system, and many like it, and granted the ability to know their design. It now works tirelessly, when it has enough fuel and material, recreating the vast planetary systems as they appear to it. The claw-mounted crane dreams of celestial bodies and is driven to reproduce them in all their glory as best a claw-mounted crane can.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
eventually making what appears to be a model of a planetary system.

a planetary system

My mind leaped ahead and I upvoted right away. Now to actually finish the article

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: this is honestly become one of all time top

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Quite interesting. Space in general is interesting.
One hundred points (up votes? Thumbs up?)! I really enjoyed the article and wanted to congratulate the author on such an interesting SCP. On to two hundred!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I pictured a sad "Wall-E" type scene where the machine longingly looked at the sky then sadly lowered its claw to continue creating a model.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I loved the dyson ring reference. I always love the "Someone is out there" SCPs.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is bordering on too lengthy for an article for its type (IE conceptually simple).

…it's five paragraphs long. I'm neutral on it, but it's hardly 'bordering on too lengthy', and I fail to see how it would be cut down.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is bordering on too lengthy for an article for its type (IE conceptually simple).

Still gets an upvote from me though. There's just… something about a machine looking up to the stars and expressing itself with its inscrutable machine motives in whatever way it can.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: fucking spheres

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Niven Ring, not Dyson. Dyson made a sphere, Niven made a Ringworld

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Nice and simple.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Everybody loves the space elevator. Personally, I think the Dyson Ring is cooler.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Not bad at all. It's kind of adorable, to be honest. By the end, I was actually wondering what was anomalous about it, until I remembered it runs on its own.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Furthermore, you don't have to say "or both". It has to run out of one or the other first, and if it ran out of both at exactly the same time, that would still satisfy the "fuel or materials" condition.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
not exactly ground breaking but, it is interesting and a good read.
great job.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Was planning to upvote this anyway on the strength of the galaxy-mapping crane arm, then got to the space elevator :)

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: That's actually a typo, and I'll fix that now. :P

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
oh shit we still have it? I did a wiki search and got a number that was being used by something else, so I assumed the forces of evil had finally tore it down.

(that's right, everyone who has different opinions from me is part of the forces of evil. I went there.)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I second that, too repetetitititive.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
automated Katamari machine.

NOT AGAIN.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
So I see the picture and I immediately get a sense of Orky pride, just wondering why it's not painted red and how many boyz I can pile into it.

Then I start reading the effect and think it's an automated Katamari machine.

Then I finish reading and realize it's an automated Katamari machine that's making scale models of various solar systems.

Then I see that one's apparently got a Dyson sphere/ring thingie, a space elevator and potentially ginormous artificial satellites.

This thing is glorious.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: When an article contains the words “tanks of red, blue, green, and yellow paint”, you can tell it'll probably be upvote-worthy…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Yeah, that's the one I was thinking of too. While the orrerries are definitely prettier, I think this is a better take on the concept. Upvote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
.. SCP-720 will activate, and remain active until it exhausts its fuel, its workable material, or both. It will remain in this state until it runs out of fuel or exhausts the available material.

Bit of repetition there.

Overall though, cool little object. Not freaky, but definitely anomalous.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Not too exciting, but not bad job. I like the space elevator.
+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Great picture, and good use of it. Upvoted.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I liked it better than what I put for that bit.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It's more just a writer's niche of mine. (that Roget was gracious enough to not erase)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: No strong feelings on this one. However, I have to say that the "number (n), number (n)" format is kind of distracting, and isn't really necessary to maintain good tone and style. Substituting "three 20-liter fuel tanks" and other such phrases would be nice.

item.title: Re: SPACE
item.content:encoded:
Aaaand another hull breach. Let's all give a big hand to the test subjects of Sphere Eighteen for bravely uncovering the company-wide conspiracy, which is that there's no air in space. Once again: We're in space. It's not a secret. I am sincerely regretting my decision not to install windows in this thing.

Cave Johnson, we're done here.

item.title: Re: Spaaaaaaaace
item.content:encoded: I'm.. actually not too sure. Uh, I have to imagine it changes, based on the amount of material available.

item.title: Spaaaaaaaace
item.content:encoded:
I know roget made that joke already, but there's more than enough space to go around.

One question though: What's the scale/size of these models? Other than that, I liked it.

Upvoted.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Don't worry, Sorts. This can make enough solar system models for at least three SCPs.

EDIT: Did you mean SCP-341?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: We used to have an SCP that was just a collection of solar system models, it was deleted and I miss it but this is a pretty good replacement for it.

item.title: SPACE
item.content:encoded: spaaaaace

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Ah, I didn't catch the Dyson Ring bit. Makes much more sense now. But yes, I love space/related skips. Always fun.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The structures consist of a Dyson Ring, Space Elevator sort of thing, and man-made satellites, yes. SCP-720 wouldn't be able to make objects small enough relative to the planets without making them tiny as hell, and since it doesn't have that kind of precision, it settled for a different shape.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I like this. Upvoted.

Question, though. I assume that the system mentioned in the addendum has an earth-like planet, along with a Space-Elevator sort of thing, and the cubic objects are crude satellites? Crude as in "made to within the best abilities of the SCP".

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Fair enough. Sometimes it's the small, quiet objects that get to you the most, and not the big Keters.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: We weren't exactly going for groundbreaking. The site needs a few more SCPs that aren't trying to be the next big thing.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: A decent, interesting SCP, if nothing groundbreaking.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: A collaboration between me and Roget.
